_metadata.description: Draft / candidate question bank for IQMaxxer. Not wired into the live quiz and not seeded into Supabase. Preserves accepted candidate items in the repo.
notes: ABS-MAT-003 v2 accepted as Abstract Reasoning Micro-Batch 1 candidate. Proof image must be regenerated later.

items.subskill: Matrix rule integration with a parametric (state-modulating) rule
items.difficulty_rationale: Two rules that are not separable: an arrow rotates clockwise across each row (Rule A), but the SIZE of each turn is set by a second feature in the cell — the number of bars beneath the dial — at bars x 45 degrees (Rule B). Because the bar count differs from cell to cell, the rotation step is different in every row, so the arrow's path cannot be predicted by any fixed-step reading; Rule A is uncomputable without first reading Rule B. This is a genuine modulating interaction (one rule parameterises the other) rather than two rules resolving in parallel, which is what made v1 too easy. It sits clearly above v1's independent Latin-square + count and below ABS-MAT-004's Boolean (XOR) discovery: there is no logical operator to invent, only a parametric coupling to notice, placing it at the upper-middle of the rule-token/solution-rate band.
items.prompt: Which option completes the 3 by 3 pattern?
items.solution: Each cell is a round dial with a single arrow pointing in one of eight compass directions (N, NE, E, SE, S, SW, W, NW = 45-degree steps clockwise from up), plus a small group of 1 to 3 bars under the dial. Two rules interact. Rule A: along each row the arrow rotates CLOCKWISE. Rule B: the number of bars in a cell sets how far the arrow turns when LEAVING that cell — turn = (bars) x 45 degrees. So the magnitude of each rotation is read off the bar count, not assumed. Verify on the given rows: Row 1 N(1 bar)->+45->NE(3 bars)->+135->S. Row 2 E(2 bars)->+90->S(1 bar)->+45->SW. Row 3 SE(3 bars)->+135->W(2 bars)->+90->? . The final step leaves the (3,2) cell, which has 2 bars, so the turn is 2 x 45 = 90 degrees clockwise: W rotated 90 degrees clockwise is N (straight up). The answer is an arrow pointing up: tile A. (Note: the bars govern only the OUTGOING turn, so the bottom-right cell's own bar count is irrelevant and the option tiles therefore differ only in arrow direction.)

distractor_rationales.A: Correct: the (3,2) cell has 2 bars, so the arrow turns 2 x 45 = 90 degrees clockwise from W, giving N (up). Requires both rules — clockwise rotation AND reading the bar count to size the turn.
distractor_rationales.B: NE (up-right): Wrong Principle — found the rotation but treated the step as a fixed turn, reusing the 135-degree step seen earlier in row 3 (W + 135 = NE). This is where a solver who never realises the bar count sets the step size lands.
distractor_rationales.C: S (down): Wrong Principle — read the bar count correctly (magnitude 90 degrees) but rotated the wrong way, anti-clockwise, giving W - 90 = S.
distractor_rationales.D: NW (up-left): Incomplete Correlate — noticed the arrow turns clockwise but ignored that the bar count scales the turn, applying a single minimum 45-degree step (W + 45 = NW).

visual_asset_spec.main_figure_description: A 3x3 grid (cell 150x150, gap 12, light grid-line borders #D4D8E0, white ground), tokens from design-tokens.json. In each cell: (1) a DIAL = a thin circle (stroke 2.5 ink #14181F) centred at cell-local (75,60), radius 40; (2) inside it a bold ARROW (stroke 2.5, round cap/join, ink) from the dial centre (75,60) to a tip on the perimeter, with a small solid triangular arrowhead (~12px) at the tip — the arrow stays entirely inside the dial; (3) beneath the dial a COUNT BAND = a horizontal row of 1 to 3 small solid bars (each 8 wide x 20 tall, ink fill, 8px gap), centred on x=75, occupying y 110-132, never overlapping the dial. Arrow direction is given as a compass point, angle measured CLOCKWISE from straight up: N=0, NE=45, E=90, SE=135, S=180, SW=225, W=270, NW=315; tip = (75 + 40*sin(angle), 60 - 40*cos(angle)). Contents by (row,col) as DIRECTION / bars: (1,1) N / 1; (1,2) NE / 3; (1,3) S / 2; (2,1) E / 2; (2,2) S / 1; (2,3) SW / 3; (3,1) SE / 3; (3,2) W / 2; (3,3) EMPTY cell containing only a muted grey '?' (#9AA2B1), no dial, no bars. Keep all dials, arrow weights and bar sizes identical so only direction and bar count vary.

option_descriptions.A: A dial with an arrow pointing straight up (N). No bars.
option_descriptions.B: A dial with an arrow pointing up and to the right (NE). No bars.
option_descriptions.C: A dial with an arrow pointing straight down (S). No bars.
option_descriptions.D: A dial with an arrow pointing up and to the left (NW). No bars.

visual_asset_spec.mobile_behavior: Scale grid to container width; arrow stroke, arrowhead and bars are sized in viewBox units so they stay crisp and >=1px at ~360px render width. Option tiles render in a 2-column grid below ~420px. Zoom enabled, because eight arrow directions must be distinguishable — edge-pointing arrows (N/E/S/W) land on a dial edge midpoint and corner-pointing arrows (NE/SE/SW/NW) land between them, so the bold arrowhead is what separates adjacent directions on a small screen. Option tiles deliberately show the dial and arrow only (no count band), because the bars are an operator on the outgoing turn and the answer cell has no successor cell.
visual_asset_spec.alt_text: A three by three grid of round dials, each showing an arrow pointing in some direction with a small group of bars beneath it; the bottom-right cell is empty with a question mark.

items.construct_rationale: Multi-rule 3x3 matrices are the canonical measure of fluid/inductive reasoning. A parametric rule, where one cell attribute sets the magnitude of another attribute's transformation, taxes rule induction plus the working-memory step of carrying a variable operator across the row — the integration load Carpenter, Just & Shell identified as the driver of matrix difficulty. Because the operator changes per cell, the item resists the surface heuristic (assume a fixed transformation) that lets weaker solvers pass two-independent-rule matrices.
items.knowledge_requirements: None beyond counting to three, recognising eight compass directions of an arrow, and the idea that an arrow can turn.

ambiguity_review.notes: Rotation direction: every observed transition is consistent with CLOCKWISE by (source-cell bars x 45 degrees) and inconsistent with anti-clockwise (e.g. N->NE in row 1 is +45 CW, not -45), so direction is fixed; the AC reading is captured as distractor C. Step magnitude: the per-row steps are (45,135), (90,45), (135,90) — there is no single constant step across the matrix, so a fixed-step heuristic cannot fit all visible cells and provides no consistent value; the only reading that fits every transition is bars x 45. Checked for a coincidental path to the key: a fixed-step solver measuring the one visible step in row 3 (SE->W = +135) and repeating it gives NE (B), not N; a unit-step solver gives NW (D); a correct-magnitude-but-reversed solver gives S (C). N is reachable only by correctly reading the (3,2) bar count (2) and rotating CW 90 degrees, i.e. only by using both rules together. The bottom-right cell's own bar count is logically inert (no outgoing step), so options vary by arrow direction alone and exactly one option (A, arrow up) satisfies the combined rule.

source_basis: Two-rule matrix construction and rule taxonomy; rotation as a figural rule (Carpenter, Just & Shell 1990)
Rule-token count and rule type as primary drivers of matrix difficulty (Primi 2001; Embretson 1998)
Raven-style distractor taxonomy — Repetition / Wrong Principle / Incomplete Correlate — used to map each option to a specific partial-rule error

items.subskill: Matrix rule integration with a Boolean operation (XOR)
items.difficulty_rationale: Two rules: an exclusive-or (XOR) on the inner marks of each row, plus an independent distribution-of-three (Latin square) on the frame shape. Discovering the XOR is the genuinely demanding step, and the OR and AND distractors make the Boolean reading discriminating. However, the two rules resolve in PARALLEL rather than modulating each other — the frame can be solved entirely on its own (Latin square) and the marks entirely on their own (XOR), and neither depends on the other's value. The XOR is also demonstrated cleanly and identically in both prior rows, so by the test cell the operation has been shown twice before it must be applied. That puts it at the top of Difficulty 4 — clearly harder than a count-or-rotation matrix because of the non-obvious logical operation, but short of true Difficulty 5, which would require an interacting/parametric rule, an operation that is not pre-demonstrated, or a higher rule-token load.
items.prompt: Which option completes the 3 by 3 pattern?
items.solution: Each cell has a FRAME (circle, square or diamond) and up to three small filled squares in three fixed interior slots (left, centre, right). Rule 1 (inner marks): in every row, the third cell shows exactly the slots that are filled in the first cell OR the second cell but NOT both — an exclusive-or. Row 3 has first cell with left+centre filled and second cell with centre+right filled; the slots filled in exactly one of them are LEFT and RIGHT (centre is filled in both, so it cancels). Rule 2 (frame): each row contains a circle, a square and a diamond once, arranged as a Latin square; row 3 already shows a diamond and a circle, so the missing frame is a SQUARE. The answer is a square frame with the left and right slots filled and the centre empty: tile C.

distractor_rationales.A: Square frame with all three slots filled: computes the inclusive-OR (or the union) instead of the exclusive-or — the single most common Boolean error.
distractor_rationales.B: Square frame with only the centre slot filled: the slot that XOR actually cancels; results from taking the AND (the overlap) instead of the XOR.
distractor_rationales.C: Correct: square frame (forced by the Latin square) with left and right filled, centre empty (the XOR of the row's first two cells).
distractor_rationales.D: Circle frame with left and right filled: solves the XOR correctly but ignores the frame rule, repeating an adjacent frame (incomplete correlate).

visual_asset_spec.main_figure_description: A 3x3 grid (cell 150x150, gap 12, light borders, white ground). Each cell shows a large centred FRAME outline (stroke 2.5, ink) — a circle, a square, or a diamond — about 92px across. Inside each frame are three fixed horizontal SLOT positions (left, centre, right), each either a small filled square (~20px, ink) when filled or empty. Encode filled slots as a triple [left,centre,right] with 1=filled, 0=empty. Contents by (row,col) as FRAME / triple: (1,1) circle /[1,0,1]; (1,2) square /[0,1,1]; (1,3) diamond /[1,1,0]; (2,1) square /[1,1,0]; (2,2) diamond /[1,0,0]; (2,3) circle /[0,1,0]; (3,1) diamond /[1,1,0]; (3,2) circle /[0,1,1]; (3,3) EMPTY cell with a muted grey '?' only. The diamond frame is a square rotated 45 degrees. Keep all three frame types optically similar in width so the inner slots align.

option_descriptions.A: A square frame with all three interior slots filled.
option_descriptions.B: A square frame with only the centre slot filled.
option_descriptions.C: A square frame with the left and right slots filled and the centre empty.
option_descriptions.D: A circle frame with the left and right slots filled and the centre empty.

visual_asset_spec.mobile_behavior: Scale grid to container; inner slots remain legible because they are sized in viewBox units. Options render 2-up below ~420px with zoom enabled (this is the hardest item to read on a phone, so zoom matters).
visual_asset_spec.alt_text: A three by three grid of framed shapes with small marks inside them; the bottom-right cell is empty with a question mark.

items.construct_rationale: An XOR-over-elements matrix with a second independent distribution rule is one of the stronger figural items in this set: it taxes rule induction and the discovery of a non-obvious logical operation, then asks the solver to hold two rules at once. The two rules are independent rather than interacting, and the XOR is pre-demonstrated in the earlier rows, so it represents a strong upper-Difficulty-4 item rather than a ceiling-level one.
items.knowledge_requirements: None beyond recognizing presence/absence of marks and three frame shapes. No knowledge of the word 'XOR' is required or shown.

ambiguity_review.notes: Verified the XOR holds in rows 1 and 2 as well: row1 [1,0,1] XOR [0,1,1] = [1,1,0] (matches cell 1,3); row2 [1,1,0] XOR [1,0,0] = [0,1,0] (matches cell 2,3). So the rule is consistently demonstrated before the test cell. Confirmed the frame arrangement is a Latin square forcing 'square' for (3,3). Checked OR and AND readings: OR gives [1,1,1] (tile A), AND gives [0,1,0] (tile B) — both are present as distractors and both are wrong, so the item discriminates XOR from the two adjacent Boolean operations. Exactly one option satisfies both rules. Difficulty note: the two rules resolve in parallel (the frame and the marks are independently solvable), and the XOR is shown twice before the test cell, which is why this is rated Difficulty 4 rather than 5.

source_basis: Boolean (exclusive-or) figural rules in advanced matrix items (Mensa Norway item progression; Carpenter, Just & Shell 1990 rule taxonomy)
Rule-token count as a primary driver of matrix difficulty, r approximately -0.57 with solution rate (Carpenter, Just & Shell 1990)

items.difficulty_rationale: A single transformation (90 degrees clockwise rotation) applied to an asymmetric figure. Distractors cover the two most common rotation errors (over-rotation to 180, and reflection-vs-rotation confusion), lifting it above a trivial one-step item without adding a second rule.
items.prompt: The first figure changes into the second in a particular way. Apply the same change to the third figure. Which option is the result?
items.solution: The change from the first figure to the second is a 90 degrees clockwise rotation: the small tab moves from the top edge to the right edge. Applying a 90 degrees clockwise rotation to the third figure (tab on the left edge) moves the tab to the top edge. That is tile B.

distractor_rationales.A: Tab on the right edge: results from rotating counter-clockwise, or from reflecting the figure instead of rotating it.
distractor_rationales.B: Correct. A 90 degrees clockwise rotation maps a left-edge tab to the top edge.
distractor_rationales.C: Tab on the bottom edge: results from over-rotating by 180 degrees.
distractor_rationales.D: Tab on the left edge: no transformation applied; simply repeats the orientation of the third figure.

visual_asset_spec.main_figure_description: A single horizontal stimulus strip on a white ground. Left group: figure 1 is a square (outline, stroke 2.5, ink) with a small solid filled triangular tab attached to the OUTSIDE of its TOP edge, centred; then a rightward arrow (stroke 2.5); then figure 2 is the same square with the solid tab attached to the OUTSIDE of its RIGHT edge, centred. A vertical divider of two stacked dots (::) separates the groups. Right group: figure 3 is the same square with the solid tab attached to the OUTSIDE of its LEFT edge, centred; then a rightward arrow; then a question-mark box (square outline with rounded corners, a '?' in muted grey centred). Squares are 96x96 with the tab ~28 wide x ~18 deep. Maintain consistent stroke and equal spacing.

option_descriptions.A: Square with the solid triangular tab on the RIGHT edge.
option_descriptions.B: Square with the solid triangular tab on the TOP edge.
option_descriptions.C: Square with the solid triangular tab on the BOTTOM edge.
option_descriptions.D: Square with the solid triangular tab on the LEFT edge.

visual_asset_spec.mobile_behavior: Scale to container width; strip may wrap the two groups onto two lines below ~420px while keeping each figure's internal proportions and stroke clarity.
visual_asset_spec.alt_text: A square with a small tab on one edge, an arrow, the same square with the tab on a different edge, then a third square with a tab and a question mark.

items.construct_rationale: Figure analogies (A:B::C:?) require inferring a transformation from one pair and applying it to a new instance — a core inductive-reasoning operation in the ICAR and Raven figure-item tradition. The asymmetric tab makes orientation trackable without relying on knowledge.
items.knowledge_requirements: None beyond recognizing that a shape can be rotated.

ambiguity_review.notes: The competing rule is reflection. Reflection across the vertical axis maps a top tab to a top tab (unchanged); across the horizontal axis maps top to bottom. Neither reproduces the observed top-to-right mapping in the A-to-B example. Only a 90 degrees clockwise rotation does. The example therefore uniquely fixes the rule as rotation, and a left tab maps only to the top. Single answer holds.

source_basis: Figure-analogy A:B::C:? structure as used in ICAR and Raven-style figure items (Condon & Revelle 2014)
Rotation as a transformation rule; reflection-vs-rotation distractor design (Carpenter, Just & Shell 1990)

items.difficulty_rationale: Three figures are rotations of one chiral shape; one is its mirror image, which cannot be produced by rotation. Solvers must detect handedness (chirality), a transformation that is reliably missed when people check only gross orientation. The 180-degree rotation acts as a 'looks flipped' decoy.
items.prompt: Three of these figures are rotations of the same shape. One is not. Which figure does NOT belong?
items.solution: Tiles A, B and D are all rotations of the same F-shaped pentomino (at 0, 180 and 90 degrees clockwise). Tile C is the MIRROR image of that shape. A mirror image is a reflection, not a rotation: no amount of turning in the plane converts a left-handed F into a right-handed F. Therefore tile C does not belong.

distractor_rationales.A: Belongs: the base orientation of the F-pentomino.
distractor_rationales.B: Belongs: the base shape rotated 180 degrees. This is the most common wrong choice because it looks 'flipped', but it is a genuine rotation.
distractor_rationales.C: Correct (the odd one): a mirror reflection of the shape, which has the opposite handedness and cannot be obtained by rotation.
distractor_rationales.D: Belongs: the base shape rotated 90 degrees clockwise.

visual_asset_spec.main_figure_description: There is no separate stimulus; the four answer tiles ARE the figures to compare, shown as a labelled set (A, B, C, D). Each tile is a 132x132 white box (rounded corners, hairline grid-line border) containing an F-pentomino drawn as five filled unit cells (cell ~26px, ink fill, hairline internal separators optional) on an implicit 3x3 grid, centred. Cell maps (col,row, origin top-left): TILE A (F base): (1,0),(2,0),(0,1),(1,1),(1,2). TILE B (F rotated 180): (1,0),(2,1),(1,1),(0,2),(1,2). TILE C (mirror F): (0,0),(1,0),(1,1),(2,1),(1,2). TILE D (F rotated 90 clockwise): (1,0),(0,1),(1,1),(2,1),(2,2). Draw all four identically in stroke and fill so only shape differs.

option_descriptions.A: An F-pentomino in its base orientation.
option_descriptions.C: The mirror image of the pentomino.
option_descriptions.D: The same pentomino rotated 90 degrees clockwise.

visual_asset_spec.mobile_behavior: Reflow the four tiles into a 2x2 grid below ~420px; keep each pentomino's cells crisp at small size by preserving integer cell sizing.
visual_asset_spec.alt_text: Four tiles, each containing a shape made of five connected squares in different orientations.

items.construct_rationale: Odd-one-out over rotations versus a reflection isolates the ability to abstract an invariant (handedness) across surface transformations — a fluid-reasoning operation. It deliberately does not depend on labels or knowledge.
items.knowledge_requirements: None. The term 'pentomino' is not shown to the user; only the figures appear.

ambiguity_review.notes: Checked that the chosen shape is genuinely chiral: the F-pentomino has rotational symmetry of order 1 (four distinct rotations) and its mirror is not among those four rotations, so exactly one tile (the mirror) is unrotatable into the others. Verified each of A, B, D is a distinct true rotation of the same base and that C is the reflection. No second figure qualifies as the odd one.

source_basis: Mental-rotation and chirality detection (Shepard & Metzler 1971)
Classification / odd-one-out inductive item format (ICAR and Raven tradition; Primi 2001 on perceptual encoding load)

items.subskill: Applying a specified rotation to an asymmetric figure and rejecting the reflection foil
items.difficulty_rationale: A single named transformation — a quarter-turn (90 degrees) clockwise — applied to one clear, doubly-asymmetric figure (a pennant flag: it is asymmetric top-to-bottom AND left-to-right, so both rotation amount and handedness are trackable). The three foils are the three canonical rotation errors: wrong direction (90 CCW), over-rotation (180), and a mirror image. The mirror foil lifts it just above a trivial one-step turn, but because the required angle is stated and the figure has few parts, it stays at the easy end. No second rule, no in-figure detail.
items.prompt: The figure on the left is rotated a quarter-turn clockwise (90 degrees). Which option shows the result?
items.solution: The figure is a vertical pole with a small solid pennant at the TOP pointing RIGHT and a solid round knob at the BOTTOM. A quarter-turn clockwise sends 'top' to 'right' and 'bottom' to 'left', so the pole becomes horizontal with the knob at the LEFT end and the pennant at the RIGHT end. The pennant, which pointed right (away from the pole, toward the direction that 'top' moves into), now points DOWN. The result is a horizontal pole, knob on the left, pennant on the right end pointing down: tile A.

distractor_rationales.A: Correct: 90 degrees clockwise — pole horizontal, knob left, pennant on the right end pointing down.
distractor_rationales.B: Pole horizontal, pennant on the LEFT end pointing up, knob on the right: this is a 90-degree turn the wrong way (counter-clockwise). The most common direction error.
distractor_rationales.C: Pole vertical, knob at TOP, pennant at the BOTTOM pointing left: this is a half-turn (180 degrees) — over-rotation.
distractor_rationales.D: Pole vertical, pennant at the TOP pointing LEFT, knob at bottom: this is the mirror image of the original, not a rotation at all (opposite handedness). Catches solvers who flip rather than turn.

visual_asset_spec.main_figure_description: Tokens from design-tokens.json: ink #14181F line art, stroke 2.5 (round cap/join), white ground, no accent needed. STIMULUS (left of the option row, or above on mobile): one 150x150 tile containing the target flag. Geometry in tile-local coords (0..150): POLE = vertical line from (75,35) to (75,115), stroke 2.5. PENNANT = a small solid filled triangle attached at the top of the pole, pointing right: vertices (75,35),(110,46),(75,57). KNOB = solid filled circle radius 7 centred at (75,115). Then a rightward arrow (stroke 2.5) separating stimulus from a question-mark box (rounded square, muted grey '?' #9AA2B1). OPTION TILES are each 150x150 with the same line weights; each shows the same flag (pole + one pennant + one knob, identical sizes) in a different orientation. Tile A: pole HORIZONTAL from (35,75) to (115,75); knob at (35,75); pennant a solid triangle at the right end pointing DOWN, vertices (115,75),(104,110),(93,75). Tile B: pole HORIZONTAL (35,75)-(115,75); knob at (115,75); pennant at the LEFT end pointing UP, vertices (35,75),(46,40),(57,75). Tile C: pole VERTICAL (75,35)-(75,115); knob at TOP (75,35); pennant at the BOTTOM pointing LEFT, vertices (75,115),(40,104),(75,93). Tile D: pole VERTICAL (75,35)-(75,115); knob at BOTTOM (75,115); pennant at the TOP pointing LEFT, vertices (75,35),(40,46),(75,57). Keep pennant and knob sizes identical across all tiles so only orientation/handedness differs.

option_descriptions.A: Horizontal pole, knob on the left end, pennant on the right end pointing down.
option_descriptions.B: Horizontal pole, pennant on the left end pointing up, knob on the right end.
option_descriptions.C: Vertical pole, knob at the top, pennant at the bottom pointing left.
option_descriptions.D: Vertical pole, pennant at the top pointing left, knob at the bottom.

visual_asset_spec.mobile_behavior: Stimulus strip stacks above the option grid below ~420px; options render 2x2. The flag has only three large parts so it stays legible without zoom, but zoom remains enabled.
visual_asset_spec.alt_text: A flag shape made of a pole, a small triangular pennant, and a round knob, shown next to an arrow and a question mark; four option tiles each show the flag in a different orientation.

items.construct_rationale: Applying a known rigid rotation to an asymmetric figure and distinguishing it from its mirror image is the foundational mental-rotation operation (Shepard & Metzler tradition, 2D form). The doubly-asymmetric flag makes both the angle and the handedness diagnosable from a quick glance, keeping the load low.
items.knowledge_requirements: None beyond understanding 'clockwise' and that a shape can be turned.

ambiguity_review.notes: Because the angle (90 CW) is stated, the result is uniquely determined; only tile A matches it. Checked that no foil coincidentally equals the 90 CW result: B is its mirror across the horizontal (the CCW result), C is the 180 result, D is the reflection of the original — all distinct from A. The flag's double asymmetry guarantees the four orientations are pairwise distinguishable (no accidental symmetry collapses two tiles).

source_basis: Mental rotation of asymmetric figures and reflection-vs-rotation foils (Shepard & Metzler 1971)
Rotation-amount and direction errors as standard spatial distractors (Vandenberg & Kuse 1978, Mental Rotations Test)

items.subskill: Deriving opposite-face pairs from a net and rejecting cube views that show opposite faces together
items.difficulty_rationale: The solver must mentally fold a six-square net into a cube and determine which faces end up opposite each other, then test four cube views against that. The discriminating principle — two faces that are opposite can never both be visible on the same three-face view — is clean and statable, but extracting the three opposite-pairs from the net requires genuine folding visualisation. Crucially, all face marks are rotationally symmetric, so there is NO in-face orientation 'gotcha'; difficulty comes only from the folding step. That places it squarely at medium (Difficulty 3), not higher.
items.prompt: The net on the left folds into a cube. Which of the four cubes could it be?
items.solution: Fold the net. In this net the four faces in the long horizontal strip (ring, dot, plus, X — reading left to right) wrap into a band, so opposite faces are the ones two apart in the band: RING is opposite PLUS, and DOT is opposite X. The two faces above and below the strip (square on top of the dot face, ringed-dot below it) fold to the remaining pair, so SQUARE is opposite RINGED-DOT. The three opposite pairs are therefore: dot-X, ring-plus, square-ringed-dot. A valid cube view shows three MUTUALLY ADJACENT faces, i.e. no opposite pair. Tile A shows dot, ring and square — none of which are opposite to each other — so it is foldable. Each other tile shows one of the forbidden opposite pairs: B shows dot and X, C shows ring and plus, D shows square and ringed-dot. Those three are impossible. The answer is A.

distractor_rationales.A: Correct: shows dot, ring, and square — three mutually adjacent faces meeting at one corner, with no opposite pair.
distractor_rationales.B: Shows the dot face and the X face together; these are opposite faces and can never both be visible at once. Impossible.
distractor_rationales.C: Shows the ring face and the plus face together; these are opposite. Impossible.
distractor_rationales.D: Shows the square face and the ringed-dot face together; these are opposite. Impossible.

visual_asset_spec.main_figure_description: Tokens from design-tokens.json: ink line art, stroke 2.5, white ground, grid-line #D4D8E0 cell borders. NET (stimulus): six equal squares (each 64x64) arranged in a cross/T. Layout in net-local coords with cell (col,row), origin top-left of the net: a horizontal strip of FOUR cells at row 1: positions col0=RING (circle outline, r18, centred), col1=DOT (solid filled circle r12), col2=PLUS (a '+' of two crossed strokes ~36 long), col3=X (a saltire '×' ~36 long). Above col1 (row 0): SQUARE mark (a small solid filled square ~26). Below col1 (row 2): RINGED-DOT mark (a circle outline r18 with a solid dot r7 at its centre). All marks are rotationally symmetric so face orientation is irrelevant. OPTIONS: four isometric cubes, each drawn as a standard three-quarter view showing exactly three faces — a TOP face (rhombus) and two FRONT faces (left-front and right-front parallelograms), stroke 2.5, faint interior face shading optional using paper_tint. Marks are centred on each visible face, sized to read at small scale. Tile A: top=SQUARE, left-front=RING, right-front=DOT. Tile B: top=RING, left-front=DOT, right-front=X. Tile C: top=SQUARE, left-front=RING, right-front=PLUS. Tile D: top=SQUARE, left-front=RINGED-DOT, right-front=DOT. Draw all cubes at identical size and viewing angle so only the marks differ.

option_descriptions.A: A cube showing a square mark on top, a ring on the left face, and a dot on the right face.
option_descriptions.B: A cube showing a ring on top, a dot on the left face, and an X on the right face.
option_descriptions.C: A cube showing a square on top, a ring on the left face, and a plus on the right face.
option_descriptions.D: A cube showing a square on top, a ringed-dot on the left face, and a dot on the right face.

visual_asset_spec.mobile_behavior: Net stacks above the four cubes below ~420px; cubes render 2x2. Marks are large and high-contrast so they survive small render sizes; zoom enabled for the isometric faces.
visual_asset_spec.alt_text: A flat cross-shaped net of six squares each carrying a simple symbol, beside four drawings of a cube each showing three symbol-bearing faces.

items.construct_rationale: Surface development (folding a 2D net into a 3D solid and tracking face relationships) is a core spatial-visualization factor distinct from mental rotation. Using the opposite-face invariant makes the item objective and orientation-free while still requiring the fold to be performed mentally.
items.knowledge_requirements: None. No knowledge of the word 'net' or of opposite-face rules is assumed; the relationships are derivable by visualising the fold.

ambiguity_review.notes: Opposite pairs derived from the net: strip RING-DOT-PLUS-X... rechecked — strip order ring,dot,plus,X gives band-opposite pairs ring/plus and dot/X; the flap pair (square above, ringed-dot below the dot face) gives square/ringed-dot. Each of B, C, D contains exactly one of these three opposite pairs, so each is impossible regardless of the third face or any rotation. Tile A's three faces (dot, ring, square) contain no opposite pair and do meet at a common corner, so it is genuinely foldable. Because all marks are rotationally symmetric, there is no second, orientation-based reading that could invalidate A or rescue a distractor. Exactly one valid option.

source_basis: Surface development / paper-folding as a spatial-visualization factor (Thurstone 1938; Carroll 1993 spatial taxonomy)
Cube-net opposite-face reasoning as used in standard spatial test items (Shepard & Feng 1972, mental paper folding)

items.subskill: Selecting the piece set that tiles a target outline, with rotation and footprint reasoning
items.difficulty_rationale: The solver must hold a target outline in mind and test whether each candidate piece pair can tile it exactly, rotating pieces mentally and reasoning about whether a piece even fits inside the target's bounding footprint. Two of the three foils are AREA-MATCHED (they total the correct number of cells), so they cannot be dismissed by counting — the solver must realise that a straight four-in-a-line piece cannot fit inside a three-by-three footprint. The correct pair also requires actively visualising the wrap-around fit, not just elimination. That combination of mental rotation plus fit/footprint reasoning, with counting defeated, puts it at Difficulty 4 (toward the lower end of 4).
items.prompt: Which pair of pieces can be fitted together — turning them if needed, but not flipping them — to exactly fill the grey target shape, with no gaps and no overlaps?
items.solution: The target is a 3x3 square of unit cells with the top-right cell removed — eight cells total. The pieces in option A are a 2x2 square block and an L-shaped four-cell piece. Place the 2x2 block in the top-left corner (covering the four cells of the upper-left quadrant); the L-piece, rotated so its long edge runs along the bottom row and its foot rises up the right column, fills the remaining four cells (bottom row plus the middle-right cell). Together they cover all eight cells exactly. The answer is A. The other options fail: B and C are area-matched (eight cells) but contain a piece that needs a straight run of four cells, which cannot fit inside the target's three-by-three footprint; D has nine cells and overfills.

distractor_rationales.A: Correct: a 2x2 square plus a four-cell L-piece tile the eight-cell target exactly after rotating the L.
distractor_rationales.B: Area-matched (8 cells) but includes a five-cell L-piece whose straight arm is four cells long; no four-in-a-line segment fits within a 3x3 footprint, so it cannot be placed. Catches solvers who check only the cell count.
distractor_rationales.C: Area-matched (8 cells) but includes a straight four-in-a-line piece, which again cannot fit inside the 3x3 footprint. Same footprint trap as B, different piece.
distractor_rationales.D: A 2x2 square plus a five-cell P-piece totals nine cells — one too many. Overfills the eight-cell target. Catches solvers who don't total the area.

visual_asset_spec.main_figure_description: Tokens from design-tokens.json: ink outlines stroke 2.5, white ground, target filled paper_tint #F5F6F8 with ink outline; pieces filled white with ink outline and hairline internal cell separators (grid_line) so unit cells are countable. Unit cell = 34px throughout. TARGET (stimulus): a 3x3 arrangement of unit cells with the TOP-RIGHT cell omitted. Present cells by (col,row) origin top-left: (0,0),(1,0),(0,1),(1,1),(2,1),(0,2),(1,2),(2,2). Draw as one filled silhouette with the outer outline emphasised and faint internal cell lines. OPTION TILES each contain TWO separate pieces drawn side by side (small gap between them), each piece shown as connected unit cells with internal separators, in an arbitrary rotation: Tile A: piece 1 = 2x2 square block (cells (0,0),(1,0),(0,1),(1,1)); piece 2 = L-tetromino shown rotated, cells (0,0),(0,1),(0,2),(1,2) [a column of three with one cell to the right at the bottom]. Tile B: piece 1 = L-PENTOMINO, cells (0,0),(0,1),(0,2),(0,3),(1,3) [a straight arm of four with a foot] ; piece 2 = L-tromino, cells (0,0),(1,0),(1,1). Tile C: piece 1 = I-tetromino, cells (0,0),(1,0),(2,0),(3,0) [straight four-in-a-line]; piece 2 = L-tetromino, cells (0,0),(1,0),(2,0),(2,1). Tile D: piece 1 = 2x2 square block; piece 2 = P-pentomino, cells (0,0),(1,0),(0,1),(1,1),(0,2). Label each tile A-D. Keep cell size identical everywhere so areas are visually comparable.

option_descriptions.A: A 2-by-2 square block and a four-cell L-shaped piece.
option_descriptions.B: A five-cell L piece with a long straight arm, plus a small three-cell corner piece.
option_descriptions.C: A straight four-in-a-row piece and a four-cell L-shaped piece.
option_descriptions.D: A 2-by-2 square block and a five-cell P-shaped piece.

visual_asset_spec.mobile_behavior: Target stacks above the option grid below ~420px; options render 2x2. Unit-cell separators keep counts readable; zoom enabled so piece cells can be counted on a phone.
visual_asset_spec.alt_text: A grey target shape made of eight squares (a three-by-three block missing one corner), beside four options that each show two separate multi-square pieces.

items.construct_rationale: Form assembly — combining parts into a whole — is a classic spatial-visualization task (the form-board tradition). Requiring exact tiling with mental rotation, and defeating the area-count shortcut with footprint-impossible foils, forces true spatial fit reasoning rather than arithmetic.
items.knowledge_requirements: None beyond counting small squares and the idea that a piece can be turned.

ambiguity_review.notes: Target = 3x3 minus the top-right cell (8 cells). Verified A tiles it: 2x2 block in the upper-left quadrant covers (0,0),(1,0),(0,1),(1,1); the L-tetromino rotated covers (0,2),(1,2),(2,2),(2,1) — together exactly the eight target cells, no overlap, no gap, rotation only (no flip needed; the L's handedness matches). Verified each foil is impossible for a certain reason: B contains a piece with a straight arm of four cells and C contains a straight four-in-a-line piece — the target's bounding box is only 3x3, so no four-collinear-cell segment can be placed, making both impossible despite matching the 8-cell area; D totals 9 cells and cannot fit an 8-cell target. No second option can tile the target, so the answer is unique. Reflections are explicitly disallowed in the prompt, removing any handedness ambiguity.

source_basis: Form assembly / part-whole construction as a spatial-visualization measure (Thurstone 1938; Wechsler Object Assembly tradition)
Polyomino tiling and bounding-footprint constraints; area-matched non-fitting distractors to defeat counting heuristics (Carroll 1993 spatial factor)

items.technical_construct: Mental rotation (3D rigid rotation of a multi-cube object)
items.subskill: Identifying a true 3D rotation of a chiral cube figure and rejecting its mirror image (enantiomorph) and structurally different foils
items.difficulty_rationale: A Shepard-Metzler-style object: eight unit cubes joined in a chain with two right-angle bends lying in two different planes, which makes the object chiral (its mirror image cannot be rotated onto it). The solver must build the 3D structure mentally, rotate it, and decide which option is the same object. The decisive foil is the enantiomorph (option B) — the single hardest distractor in the spatial literature, because reversed handedness defeats every feature-matching or partial-rotation strategy. This is genuine ceiling spatial load, not clutter: the object is only eight cubes with two bends, so the difficulty comes from the 3D rotation-plus-handedness reasoning rather than from visual density. The exact coordinates below make the item uniquely solvable and deterministically renderable; the ONLY residual risk is mobile legibility (eight cubes across five panels). The 5 rating is therefore contingent on the rendered asset passing the post-render legibility check described in mobile_behavior and ambiguity_review. If, after rendering at the specified edge length, individual cubes are not separable on a ~360px-wide phone screen even with zoom, downgrade to Difficulty 4 — the logic stays valid, but perceptual difficulty would otherwise inflate the construct.
items.prompt: The object on the left is shown again — turned to a new position — in exactly one of the four options. The others are either a mirror image or a different object. Which option is the SAME object, only rotated?
items.solution: The target is a chain of eight cubes whose step-to-step moves are [+x,+x,+y,+y,+z,+z,+z]: a run of three cubes along +x, a turn upward for a run of three (two +y steps), then a turn into depth for a run of four (three +z steps). The two turns lie in different planes, giving the object a fixed handedness (right-handed screw sense). OPTION A is this same object after a proper rotation R_y(+90 degrees), matrix [[0,0,1],[0,1,0],[-1,0,0]] i.e. (x,y,z)->(z,y,-x), then a normalizing shift; its moves become [-z,-z,+y,+y,+x,+x,+x] — identical structure and identical handedness, so A is a true rotation and is the answer. OPTION B is the target reflected through the z=0 plane, (x,y,z)->(x,y,-z); its moves are [+x,+x,+y,+y,-z,-z,-z] — same run lengths and bend angles but reversed handedness, so it is the mirror image and cannot be produced by any rotation. OPTION C has three bends, not two (moves [+x,+x,+y,+z,+z,-y,-y]), so it is a structurally different object. OPTION D has run lengths of 2, 3 and 5 cubes (moves [+x,+y,+y,+z,+z,+z,+z], a run-step multiset of {1,2,4} versus the target's {2,2,3}), so it is also a different object. Only A preserves both the connectivity and the handedness of the target.

distractor_rationales.A: Correct: a proper rotation (R_y(+90 degrees)) of the target — same connectivity, same run lengths, same handedness.
distractor_rationales.B: The enantiomorph (mirror image through the z=0 plane). Identical run lengths and bend angles but opposite handedness; no rotation can reproduce it. The hardest foil — traps anyone who matches arm lengths without checking screw sense.
distractor_rationales.C: Structurally different: it has three right-angle bends instead of two, so it is not congruent to the target under any rigid motion.
distractor_rationales.D: Structurally different: its straight runs are 2, 3 and 5 cubes long (run-step multiset {1,2,4}) rather than the target's 3, 3 and 4 cubes ({2,2,3}), so it is a different object.

visual_asset_spec.coordinate_system: Right-handed integer cube lattice. Each tuple is the integer position of one unit cube; x = right, y = up, z = depth (away from viewer). Cubes are listed in chain order (cube 1 to cube 8); consecutive cubes differ by exactly 1 in one axis. All coordinate lists are normalized so the minimum value on each axis is 0.

transform_provenance.A_from_target: A = R_y(+90 degrees) applied to each target cube, then translate by (0,0,+2) to normalize. R_y(+90) = [[0,0,1],[0,1,0],[-1,0,0]]; equivalently (x,y,z)->(z,y,-x). det = +1 (proper rotation), so A is the SAME object reoriented.
transform_provenance.B_from_target: B = reflection M applied to each target cube, then translate by (0,0,+3). M = diag(1,1,-1); equivalently (x,y,z)->(x,y,-z), a reflection in the z=0 plane. det = -1 (improper). Because the object is chiral, B is the enantiomorph and is NOT reachable by any rotation.
transform_provenance.C_structure: C is authored directly (not a transform of the target). Move sequence [+x,+x,+y,+z,+z,-y,-y] gives THREE right-angle bends, versus the target's two. Bend count is a rigid-motion invariant, so C is a different object.
transform_provenance.D_structure: D is authored directly. Move sequence [+x,+y,+y,+z,+z,+z,+z] gives straight runs of 2, 3 and 5 cubes (run-step multiset {1,2,4}) versus the target's runs of 3, 3 and 4 cubes ({2,2,3}). Run-length multiset is a rigid-motion invariant, so D is a different object.

visual_asset_spec.projection: Render every cube with a single fixed isometric projection — do NOT apply any additional render-time rotation; each option's orientation is fully encoded in its coordinates. For a lattice point (x,y,z): screenX = ORIGIN_X + (x - z) * 0.8660 * E; screenY = ORIGIN_Y + (x + z) * 0.5 * E - y * E, where E = cube edge in px (use E = 28). Draw each unit cube as the standard isometric hexagon: a top rhombus plus a left and a right side-face. Painter's order: the view direction is from (+1,+1,+1) toward the origin, so sort cubes by ascending (x + y + z) and draw in that order, letting nearer cubes (larger x + y + z) paint last (on top).
visual_asset_spec.shading_and_stroke: Tokens from design-tokens.json. Cube edges: ink #14181F, stroke 2 (round join). Face fills, three flat tones for solidity: top faces #FFFFFF (paper), left faces #F5F6F8 (paper_tint), right faces #E7E9EE (a light grey between paper_tint and fill_muted). No accent colour needed. Outline the full silhouette of each object slightly heavier (stroke 2.5) so the chain reads as one solid against the white ground.
visual_asset_spec.main_figure_description: STIMULUS: the 'target' object drawn alone at the top (or left) using object_coordinates.target and the projection above. Then a divider, then the four option tiles. OPTION TILES: each a fixed-size panel (recommend 180x200) rendering, respectively, object_coordinates.A, .B, .C, .D, each centred in its panel using the SAME E, the SAME projection, and the SAME painter's rule. Label tiles A, B, C, D. Every cube must show all of its visible edges; where one cube partially hides another, keep the front cube's edges crisp so the count of eight cubes stays recoverable.

option_descriptions.A: An eight-cube chain with two right-angle bends, drawn from object_coordinates.A.
option_descriptions.B: An eight-cube chain with two right-angle bends, drawn from object_coordinates.B.
option_descriptions.C: An eight-cube chain with three right-angle bends, drawn from object_coordinates.C.
option_descriptions.D: An eight-cube chain with two right-angle bends and different run lengths, drawn from object_coordinates.D.

visual_asset_spec.mobile_behavior: Stimulus stacks above the option grid below ~420px; options render 2x2. This is the most demanding item to read on a phone, so zoom is mandatory and enabled by default on this item. Occlusion mitigation: (1) the fixed (+1,+1,+1) camera plus the chosen coordinates keep every chain spread across the projection rather than stacked toward the viewer, so no cube is fully hidden; (2) full per-cube edge outlining and the three-tone face shading keep adjacent cubes separable; (3) options are never re-rotated at render time, so the apparent orientation is exactly the coordinates. POST-RENDER CHECK (required before acceptance): rasterize each tile at 360px panel width and confirm all eight cubes are countable and the silhouette of A is distinguishable from B; if not, increase E or the panel size, and if it still fails, downgrade the item to Difficulty 4.
visual_asset_spec.alt_text: A three-dimensional object built from eight small cubes joined in a bent chain, shown beside four other cube-chain objects drawn at different orientations.

items.construct_rationale: Rotating a three-dimensional asymmetric object in the mind and distinguishing it from its enantiomorph is the canonical ceiling measure of spatial ability (Shepard & Metzler; Vandenberg & Kuse Mental Rotations Test). It simultaneously loads 3D structure-building, rotation, and handedness checking. Exact lattice coordinates and a single fixed projection remove the viewing-angle ambiguity that weakened version 1.
items.knowledge_requirements: None beyond perceiving a line drawing as a 3D object and understanding the difference between 'turned' and 'mirror image'.

ambiguity_review.notes: Proofs use rigid-motion invariants on the move sequences (target moves M_T = [+x,+x,+y,+y,+z,+z,+z]). (A is a true rotation) A = R_y(+90)·target with det = +1; a proper rotation maps an object to a congruent copy of the SAME handedness, so A is the target reoriented. (B is a mirror, not a rotation) B = diag(1,1,-1)·target with det = -1. Suppose a rotation R mapped target onto B reading the chain in the same order: R would have to satisfy R(+x)=+x, R(+y)=+y, R(+z)=-z, i.e. R = diag(1,1,-1), which has det = -1 and is not a rotation — contradiction. Reading B's chain in reverse order gives moves [+z,+z,+z,-y,-y,-x,-x]; matching target's forward moves would force R(+x) to equal both +z and -y, impossible. So no rotation (under either chain orientation) maps target to B; B is the enantiomorph. (C is structurally different) C has three right-angle bends versus the target's two; bend count is invariant under all rigid motions, so C is not congruent to the target. (D is structurally different) D's straight-run lengths are 2, 3, 5 cubes (run-step multiset {1,2,4}) versus the target's 3, 3, 4 cubes ({2,2,3}); the run-length multiset is invariant under rigid motions, so D is not congruent to the target. (Exactly one valid) Of the four, only A is congruent-by-rotation to the target: B is a reflection, C and D are not congruent at all. Therefore exactly one option is correct. Residual risk is perceptual only (mobile occlusion), addressed by the required post-render legibility check in mobile_behavior; it does not affect single-answer uniqueness.

source_basis: 3D mental rotation and enantiomorph (mirror) foils (Shepard & Metzler 1971)
Mirror-image versus rotated-image discrimination as the ceiling spatial item type (Vandenberg & Kuse 1978, Mental Rotations Test; Carroll 1993 spatial taxonomy)

items.subskill: Identifying a recursive generating rule and extending it one term
items.difficulty_rationale: A short series with one consistent recursive rule (each term is double the previous plus one) that is independently confirmed by the first-difference pattern (3, 6, 12, 24 — each difference doubles). Two natural, low-complexity rule families converge on the same next term, so the answer is well-determined while the reasoning is a single step of rule induction. Light arithmetic, no large numbers. Easy end of the range.
items.solution: Each term is double the previous term plus one: 2x2+1=5, 5x2+1=11, 11x2+1=23, 23x2+1=47, so the next term is 47x2+1 = 95. The same answer falls out of the differences (3, 6, 12, 24), which double each step: the next difference is 48, and 47+48 = 95. Both readings agree, so the answer is 95.

distractor_rationales.B: 94: applied 'double the previous term' but dropped the +1 (47x2).
distractor_rationales.C: 71: continued the differences with the last difference unchanged (47 + 24) instead of doubling it to 48.
distractor_rationales.D: 96: doubled and added 2 instead of 1 (47x2+2), a slip in the additive constant.

visual_asset_spec.main_figure_description: No figure. Render the sequence inline in the prompt using the UI numeric font; place the five given terms and the '?' on one line separated by commas with comfortable spacing. Optionally render the '?' in the muted grey token (#9AA2B1) to match the question-mark styling used elsewhere.
visual_asset_spec.mobile_behavior: Pure text; the sequence fits one line at mobile width. Options render as four tappable number chips, 2 columns on mobile.
visual_asset_spec.alt_text: A sequence of five numbers followed by a question mark, with four numeric answer choices.

items.construct_rationale: Number-series completion is a standard marker of inductive reasoning applied to quantity (the ICAR / Thurstone number-series tradition). Using a rule that is corroborated by two independent readings keeps the answer objective.
items.knowledge_requirements: None beyond doubling and adding small whole numbers.

ambiguity_review.notes: Uniqueness argument (over plausible low-complexity rules): the rule a(n+1)=2a(n)+1 fits all four observed transitions exactly, and the equivalent first-difference rule (differences 3,6,12,24, each x2) also fits and predicts the same next difference (48). Both give 95. Common competing simple rules are ruled out by the data: a constant difference fails (differences are not constant), a constant ratio fails (5/2, 11/5, ... are not equal), and add-a-fixed-number fails. While any finite series can be fit by a high-degree polynomial, no rule of complexity at or below the intended one yields a value other than 95; each distractor corresponds to a specific identifiable mis-step, not a rival global rule.

source_basis: Number-series items as inductive-reasoning measures (Thurstone 1938 Number factor; ICAR number series, Condon & Revelle 2014)
Recursive (multiply-and-add) generating rules with corroborating difference structure (standard quantitative-reasoning item design)

items.subskill: Inferring a two-input arithmetic rule from worked rows and applying it
items.difficulty_rationale: The solver must infer a rule that combines the two left-hand numbers in each row to produce the right-hand number, using two fully worked rows as evidence, then apply it to the third. The rule (sum of the two squares) is not the first thing most people try, and the two worked rows over-determine it — they eliminate the tempting 'product' and 'square of the sum' readings. Discovering a non-obvious two-input rule from examples is solid medium difficulty; the arithmetic itself stays small.
items.prompt:
In each row, the third number is produced from the first two by the same rule. What number replaces the question mark?

Row 1:  3,  4  ->  25
Row 2:  5,  2  ->  29
Row 3:  6,  3  ->  ?
items.solution: The third number is the sum of the squares of the first two. Row 1: 3^2 + 4^2 = 9 + 16 = 25. Row 2: 5^2 + 2^2 = 25 + 4 = 29. Both check out, so Row 3 is 6^2 + 3^2 = 36 + 9 = 45.

distractor_rationales.B: 81: squared the sum, (6+3)^2 = 81 — a common mis-reading of 'squares', and it fails on the given rows ((3+4)^2 = 49, not 25).
distractor_rationales.C: 39: squared only the first number and added the second, 6^2 + 3 = 39.
distractor_rationales.D: 18: multiplied the two numbers, 6 x 3 = 18, ignoring the squaring (and inconsistent with the worked rows).

visual_asset_spec.main_figure_description: No drawing needed. Present the three rows as a clean three-column table (first number, second number, result) or as the aligned text shown in the prompt. Use the UI numeric font; right-align numbers; render the '?' in muted grey (#9AA2B1). Keep generous row spacing so the two example rows read clearly as worked examples and the third as the unknown.
visual_asset_spec.mobile_behavior: The table is three short rows of small numbers and fits mobile width without scrolling. Options render as four number chips, 2 columns on mobile.
visual_asset_spec.alt_text: A small table with three rows; each of the first two rows shows two numbers and their result, and the third row shows two numbers and a question mark.

items.construct_rationale: Inferring a function of two inputs from worked examples is a clean inductive-reasoning task over quantity, distinct from a single-stream series because the solver must consider how two operands combine. Two worked rows make the rule identifiable rather than guessable.
items.knowledge_requirements: Squaring small whole numbers and adding. No algebra notation required.

ambiguity_review.notes: Two worked rows constrain the rule. Sum-of-squares fits both (25 and 29). Checked the natural competitors against BOTH rows: product (a*b) gives 12 and 10 (fail); square of the sum ((a+b)^2) gives 49 and 49 (fail); a^2 + b gives 13 and 27 (fail); a + b^2 gives 19 and 9 (fail); a*b + (a+b) gives 19 and 17 (fail). Only a^2 + b^2 satisfies both example rows, so the rule is identified and Row 3 = 45 is forced. Each distractor is the output of one of the eliminated rules or a partial application, i.e. a real reasoning slip rather than a rival rule that also fits the data.

source_basis: Two-operand rule induction from examples (inductive-reasoning item tradition; Carroll 1993, Induction factor)
Sum-of-squares and square-of-sum as classic confusable arithmetic rules used in pattern items

items.technical_construct: Quantitative reasoning (proportional reasoning with combined direct and inverse relations)
items.subskill: Combining a direct proportion (output) with an inverse proportion (workers) and tracking direction
items.difficulty_rationale: The solver must integrate two proportional relationships that pull in opposite directions: more fences means more time (direct), but more painters means less time (inverse). The difficulty is structural — recognising which quantity scales which way and combining them correctly — rather than computational; the numbers are deliberately small and divide cleanly. The distractors capture the three classic errors (drop one of the two scalings, or invert the worker relationship), which is what makes it a genuine Difficulty 4 reasoning item rather than an arithmetic exercise.
items.prompt: 3 painters can paint 2 fences in 4 hours. Working at the same steady rate, how many hours will 6 painters take to paint 8 fences?
items.solution: Find the rate per painter. 3 painters paint 2 fences in 4 hours, so the work rate is 2 fences / (3 painters x 4 hours) = 1/6 of a fence per painter-hour. With 6 painters the team paints 6 x 1/6 = 1 fence per hour. To paint 8 fences therefore takes 8 / 1 = 8 hours.

distractor_rationales.A: Correct: rate is 1/6 fence per painter-hour; 6 painters do 1 fence/hour; 8 fences take 8 hours.
distractor_rationales.B: 16: scaled the time up for the extra fences (8/2 = 4x, giving 16 hours) but ignored that the painters also doubled.
distractor_rationales.C: 2: accounted for doubling the painters but forgot the fences increased — computed the time for 6 painters to paint the original 2 fences.
distractor_rationales.D: 32: scaled by both ratios but treated more painters as MORE time (4 x 4 x 2) instead of less, inverting the worker relationship.

visual_asset_spec.main_figure_description: No figure. Render the problem as a single short paragraph in the UI font. No table or diagram is needed; keep numerals as digits for quick scanning.
visual_asset_spec.mobile_behavior: Two short sentences; wraps cleanly on mobile. Options render as four chips (with the word 'hours' or a unit label), 2 columns on mobile.
visual_asset_spec.alt_text: A word problem about painters, fences, and hours, with four numeric answer choices.

items.construct_rationale: Proportional reasoning that combines a direct and an inverse relationship is a core quantitative-reasoning skill (Gq) and a classic developmental marker. Keeping the arithmetic trivial isolates the reasoning: the solver is tested on structuring the relationships, not on calculation.
items.knowledge_requirements: Basic multiplication and division of small numbers and the everyday notions of rate and work. No formula memorisation.

ambiguity_review.notes: The constant-rate (man-hours) model is the standard and intended interpretation and yields a single value: time = (fences) x (painter-hours per fence) / (painters) = 8 x 6 / 6 = 8 hours. The phrase 'same steady rate' fixes the linear model and rules out start-up or diminishing-return readings. Each distractor is the result of a specific, nameable deviation (drop the painter scaling -> 16; drop the fence scaling -> 2; invert the painter scaling -> 32), so no distractor arises from an equally valid alternative model. Numbers chosen so the correct answer and all error answers are clean integers, avoiding any rounding ambiguity.

source_basis: Proportional and inverse-proportional reasoning as a quantitative-reasoning factor (Carroll 1993, Gq; Piagetian proportional-reasoning tradition)
Rate / work problems with combined direct and inverse scaling (standard quantitative aptitude item design)

items.subskill: Translating verbal numeric constraints into relations and using an ordering constraint to force a unique solution
items.difficulty_rationale: A strong Difficulty 4. The solver must translate four interlocking verbal statements into relations, combine the three equalities into a single reduced relation (h3 + 2h4 = 10), enumerate the small candidate set it allows, and then apply the remaining ordering constraint ('the third is the smallest') to eliminate all but one candidate. The integration of multiple constraints plus a side ordering condition is what lifts it clearly above a routine multi-step item. It is NOT Difficulty 5, however, because the equalities collapse cleanly to one linear relation and the search space is only four cases that can be scanned by hand; there is no irreducible system or large search to manage. Arithmetic is trivial throughout.
items.prompt:
Four houses stand in a row. Each has a different house number, and every number is a whole number from 1 to 9.

- The four numbers add up to 20.
- The first house's number equals the sum of the third and fourth.
- The second house's number is double the fourth's.
- The third house has the smallest number of the four.

What is the third house's number?
items.solution: Let the numbers be h1, h2, h3, h4. From the clues: h1 = h3 + h4 and h2 = 2 x h4. The total gives h1 + h2 + h3 + h4 = 20, so (h3 + h4) + 2h4 + h3 + h4 = 20, which simplifies to 2h3 + 4h4 = 20, i.e. h3 + 2h4 = 10. Test the few possibilities (h4 must be 1-4 to keep h3 positive): h4=4 -> h3=2, h2=8, h1=6, giving {6,8,2,4}; h4=3 -> h3=4 with set {7,6,4,3}; h4=2 -> h3=6 with set {8,4,6,2}; h4=1 -> h3=8 with set {9,2,8,1}. The last clue requires h3 to be the smallest of the four. Only the first case qualifies (2 is the smallest of 6,8,2,4); in every other case h3 is not the smallest. So h3 = 2.

distractor_rationales.A: Correct: the only assignment where the third number is also the smallest gives h3 = 2.
distractor_rationales.B: 4: comes from the h4=3 branch (numbers 7,6,4,3) — reached by solving the equalities but skipping the 'third is the smallest' check, or by reading off the fourth house in the correct solution.
distractor_rationales.C: 6: the first house's number in the correct solution (answered the wrong house), and also the h3 value of the h4=2 branch.
distractor_rationales.D: 8: the second house's number in the correct solution (answered the wrong house), and also the h3 value of the h4=1 branch.

visual_asset_spec.main_figure_description: No figure required. Render the setup sentence followed by the four constraints as a short bulleted list in the UI font, then the question. Keep each constraint on its own line for scannability. A row of four small empty boxes labelled 1st-4th could optionally be shown above the clues for orientation, but it is not necessary and should be omitted if it adds clutter on mobile.
visual_asset_spec.mobile_behavior: Four short bullet lines plus a one-line question; fits mobile width with no scrolling beyond the clue list. Options render as four number chips, 2 columns on mobile. No zoom needed.
visual_asset_spec.alt_text: A logic puzzle describing four house numbers with four numeric constraints, followed by four answer choices.

items.construct_rationale: Constraint-satisfaction over numbers measures deductive quantitative reasoning: representing relations, combining them, and using a side constraint to select among candidates. It complements the inductive items (sequence, pattern) by testing deduction rather than rule discovery, while keeping computation minimal.
items.knowledge_requirements: Addition, doubling, and comparing small whole numbers. No algebraic technique is required, though it can be solved with light algebra.

ambiguity_review.notes: Uniqueness verified by exhaustion. The equalities reduce the system to h3 + 2h4 = 10 with h1 = h3 + h4 and h2 = 2h4, all distinct whole numbers in 1-9. h4 must be 1,2,3, or 4 (h4>=5 forces h3<=0). The four candidates are: h4=4 -> {h1,h2,h3,h4} = {6,8,2,4}; h4=3 -> {7,6,4,3}; h4=2 -> {8,4,6,2}; h4=1 -> {9,2,8,1}. All four satisfy the sum, the two equalities, the 1-9 range, and distinctness. The ordering constraint 'h3 is the smallest' is satisfied ONLY by the h4=4 case (h3=2 is the minimum of {6,8,2,4}); in the other three, h3 is not the minimum. Hence the full set of constraints has exactly one solution and h3 = 2 is forced. Each distractor is a value present in the solution under a wrong question reading (h1=6, h2=8) or in a rejected branch (h3=4 when the ordering clue is ignored), so distractors reflect genuine slips, not alternative valid solutions.

source_basis: Deductive constraint-satisfaction reasoning with numeric variables (analytic/deductive reasoning tradition; Carroll 1993, Sequential Reasoning)
Use of an inequality/ordering constraint to force uniqueness in small integer systems (standard logic-puzzle item design)
